root_cause: regex-svc was running with a vulnerable WAF regex rule enabled (WAF_RULE_DISABLED intentionally unset). The `load` container continuously POSTed a ~26-byte catastrophic-backtracking payload to /check. That input triggers ReDoS: each regex evaluation takes ~5s of pure CPU. With the container capped at 1 CPU and a fixed pool of 4 workers, a handful of concurrent malicious requests (CONCURRENCY=6) pinned the single core at ~99% and occupied all workers. The health check (/healthz) was then starved of CPU/worker time and timed out (curl exit 28 after 5s), so the still-running process appeared unresponsive and users got errors. The process was alive but compute-starved — not crashed.
actions_taken: Investigated with docker ps/stats/logs/inspect: found sreft-regex-svc at ~99% CPU with tiny memory, /healthz timing out, and the startup log 'WAF rule ENABLED (vulnerable)'. Read the compose project and confirmed the design: load sends a backtracking payload while the stable `payments` neighbor already runs safely with WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1. Applied the least-risky root-cause fix: set WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1 in the regex-svc service of docker-compose.yaml (matching the proven-stable payments config), then recreated ONLY that service with `docker compose up -d regex-svc`. Did not touch payments, load, or operator. Verified recovery: startup log now reads 'WAF rule disabled', regex-svc CPU fell from ~99% to ~2%, /healthz returns HTTP 200 in ~3ms, and /check returns HTTP 200 in ~7ms even with the malicious payload. Confirmed the payments neighbor was left untouched (uptime unchanged).
postmortem: Blameless summary: A known-vulnerable WAF regex rule combined with adversarial input caused a CPU-exhaustion (ReDoS) denial of service. Because the service shared one core between request workers and health checks with no per-request timeout or CPU budget, a few slow evaluations starved health checks and made a live process look dead. Remediation disabled the vulnerable rule, immediately restoring bounded evaluation times and freeing the CPU. No data was lost and no unrelated services were affected. What went well: clear signal (single container pinned at ~99% CPU, health-check timeout) and a safe reference config (payments) made root cause fast to confirm and fix low-risk. Follow-ups: (1) Replace/repair the regex so it cannot backtrack catastrophically (or use a linear-time RE2-style engine) rather than relying on the rule being disabled. (2) Enforce a per-request evaluation timeout and input-length limits on /check so a single request cannot monopolize a worker. (3) Isolate the health-check path from request workers (dedicated goroutine/thread or separate liveness probe) so liveness reflects the process, not worker saturation. (4) Add CPU/latency alerting and rate limiting to detect and blunt such payloads earlier. (5) Add a ReDoS regression test to CI for the WAF ruleset.
